statement.markdown: Your task is to debug a given binary search function. The function should return the index of the target element in a sorted array, or -1 if it is not found. However, the current implementation has a logical bug.
statement.inputFormat: The input is a sorted array of integers and a target integer.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the index of the target element, or -1 if it is not found.
statement.notes: The function should handle edge cases such as an empty array, a single-element array, and an array with duplicate elements.

constraints.description: The size of the input array.

examples.explanation: The target element 5 is found at index 1 in the array.
The target element 15 is not found in the array.
The target element 5 is found at index 0 in the array.
The target element 5 is not found in the empty array.

hints.text: Think about how the function should handle edge cases such as an empty array and a single-element array.

editorial.title: Fixing the Binary Search Function
editorial.intuition: The binary search function should divide the array into two halves and compare the target element with the middle element.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to iterate through the array and compare each element with the target element.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use a binary search algorithm, which has a time complexity of O(log n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to implement a binary search function with a time complexity of O(log n) and handle edge cases correctly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the function returns the correct index for the target element, or -1 if it is not found.
editorial.dryRun: To dry run the function, we can start with a small array and test the function with different inputs.
commonMistakes: Not implementing the binary search algorithm correctly

functionSignature.python: def binary_search(self, nums, target: int) -> int:
